hauler.description: Hauler-class freight vessel. A civilian workhorse: slow enough to be unremarkable, roomy enough to stay employed, and rarely the wrong answer for ordinary paying cargo.

courier.description: Courier-class light freighter. Built to move smaller loads quickly and make urgent people slightly less unbearable. Best used where speed matters more than volume.

tug.description: Tug-class utility vessel. Little cargo space, limited dignity, and a genuinely useful ability to move heavier objects faster than they would manage alone.

shuttle.description: Shuttle-class passenger vessel. Built for moving people first and cargo second: quicker than a hauler, roomier than a courier, and usually full of schedules, complaints, or both.

probe.description: Probe-class sensor platform. A stationary observation unit used by every faction with enough patience to leave a sensor package somewhere useful and hope no one shoots it first.

kestrel.description: Kestrel-class UFP interceptor. Extremely fast, armed with intent, and built to arrive first, strike hard, and avoid prolonged arguments with anything heavier.

pelican.description: Pelican-class UFP logistics freighter. Functionally a hauler with state funding and mounted guns: tougher, louder, and a clear sign that the Union expects its cargo to arrive.

dragoon.description: Dragoon-class Blister interceptor. Not especially fast or graceful, but durable, aggressive, and entirely willing to keep fighting after refinement would have withdrawn.

mk-iv.description: MK-IV Arcworks interceptor. Officially an industrial tug retrofit. Practically a labor dispute with guns, improvised armor, and enough engine to make itself everyone else's problem.

mm-ix.description: MM-IX Arcworks utility platform. A prospecting and service hull retrofitted for local defense: heavier, uglier, and more useful around rigs and stations than in elegant pursuit.
